description:
You are a machine-learning engineer who must package a small set of raw CSV files so that they can be copied to another node, and then immediately verify that the package can be unpacked without data loss.

    Starting state of the computer
    --------------------------------
    1. Your shell opens in /home/user.
    2. Three tiny raw CSV files already exist:

       /home/user/raw_data/images_meta.csv  
       /home/user/raw_data/labels.csv  
       /home/user/raw_data/train_split.csv  

       Do NOT rename, move, or edit these three existing files.

    Goal
    ----
    Produce a compressed, checksummed archive, unpack it to a new location, verify that the unpack worked, and write a short four-line log file.  When you finish, the system must simultaneously satisfy ALL of the following conditions:

    A) Directory structure  
       /home/user/archive                      → must exist  
       /home/user/archive/training_data_package.tar.gz   → must exist and be a valid gzip-compressed tar archive that contains exactly one top-level directory named raw_data, and inside it the three CSV files listed above.  
       /home/user/archive/training_data_package.sha256   → must exist and contain precisely one line whose format is:  
          SHA256 (training_data_package.tar.gz) = <64-hex-character-hash>

       /home/user/prepared_data                → must exist  
       /home/user/prepared_data/raw_data/...   → must exist and contain the three CSV files whose byte-for-byte contents are identical to the originals in /home/user/raw_data.

    B) Verification log  
       A new file /home/user/compression_extraction.log must exist and have EXACTLY four lines, each on its own line (i.e., terminated by a single newline character “\n”).  They must appear in this order:

          Line 1  START_TIME=<epoch-seconds>  
          Line 2  ARCHIVE=/home/user/archive/training_data_package.tar.gz  
          Line 3  CHECKSUM_FILE=/home/user/archive/training_data_package.sha256  
          Line 4  STATUS=SUCCESS  

       • <epoch-seconds> can be any sequence of decimal digits representing the moment you began the operation.  
       • There must be no extra spaces before or after the “=” sign.  
       • There must be no blank lines before, after, or between these four lines.  
       • STATUS must be the literal word SUCCESS, uppercase.

    C) Checksums  
       1. The SHA-256 hash written inside training_data_package.sha256 must match the real SHA-256 of training_data_package.tar.gz.  
       2. After you untar the archive into /home/user/prepared_data, the three files produced there must be byte-for-byte identical to the originals in /home/user/raw_data (the automated test will compute SHA-256 on each corresponding pair).

    D) Permissions  
       All newly created files and directories must be owned by the current non-root user and writable by that user (typical default umask 002 works).

    What the automated test will check
    ----------------------------------
    1. Presence of the four paths stated above.  
    2. training_data_package.tar.gz can be listed with “tar -tzf” without error and contains exactly:  
         raw_data/  
         raw_data/images_meta.csv  
         raw_data/labels.csv  
         raw_data/train_split.csv  
    3. The single-line format of training_data_package.sha256 matches the pattern shown and its hash is correct.  
    4. The prepared_data copy is byte-identical to the original raw_data.  
    5. /home/user/compression_extraction.log has exactly four non-empty lines whose prefixes are START_TIME=, ARCHIVE=, CHECKSUM_FILE=, and STATUS=SUCCESS (in that order).  
    6. No extra files or directories named “raw_data” are created under /home/user except the two explicitly mentioned paths (/home/user/raw_data and /home/user/prepared_data/raw_data).

    Hints (not commands)
    --------------------
    • “tar -czf” is a common way to create a gzip-compressed tarball.  
    • “sha256sum” can compute a SHA-256 hash; redirect its output or use printf to match the requested format.  
    • To unpack the archive into /home/user/prepared_data while preserving the top-level directory inside the tarball, use the “-C” option of tar.  
    • Use the “diff” command or checksums to verify that the unpacked files match the originals before you write STATUS=SUCCESS.  
    • Capture the start-time once at the very beginning with “date +%s” so the value does not change during the workflow.

    Once all conditions in sections A–D are met, the task is finished.
truth:
<!--
    Ground-truth data that the public description must not reveal.  The test harness
    will create these initial files before the agent begins:

    1) /home/user/raw_data/images_meta.csv  (exact content)  
       id,height,width
       1,256,256
       2,128,128
       3,512,512
       (newline at end)

    2) /home/user/raw_data/labels.csv       (exact content)  
       id,label
       1,cat
       2,dog
       3,bird
       (newline at end)

    3) /home/user/raw_data/train_split.csv  (exact content)  
       id,split
       1,train
       2,validation
       3,test
       (newline at end)

    The test harness will later verify that:
    • training_data_package.tar.gz exists and contains the three files above under a single top-level directory named raw_data/.
    • The SHA-256 inside training_data_package.sha256 equals the real SHA-256 digest of the tar.gz file (calculated with sha256sum).
    • prepared_data/raw_data/… files are byte-identical to their counterparts in raw_data/.
    • /home/user/compression_extraction.log contains exactly:
          START_TIME=<digits>
          ARCHIVE=/home/user/archive/training_data_package.tar.gz
          CHECKSUM_FILE=/home/user/archive/training_data_package.sha256
          STATUS=SUCCESS
      where “<digits>” matches the regex ^[0-9]+$.

    The harness also checks that no extra files or directories whose names start with “raw_data” exist anywhere other than the two approved paths.

    End of secret section.
    -->
